citation: O'Malley GR, Pema P, Sweeney P, Sarwar S, Cassimatis N, Knightly JJ, Goldstein I, Patel NV. Impact of Cervical and Lumbar Spine Surgeries on National Football League (NFL) Player Performance and Return-to-Play Outcomes. Cureus. 2025;17(6):e85706.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reviewed NFL injury reports from 2005 to 2022 to identify 136 players who underwent 144 spine surgeries (77 lumbar, 67 cervical). Lumbar surgeries yielded a 61% return-to-play rate and an average PFF performance rating increase of 6.3%, while cervical surgeries produced a 47% return-to-play rate and an average performance decline of 5.8%. Players with prior lumbar surgery had a significantly higher return-to-play rate (83% vs. 56%), and significantly fewer cervical surgery patients experienced performance improvement compared to lumbar patients (22% vs. 58%).
tldr: NFL players who undergo lumbar spine surgery return to play at higher rates and may even improve performance slightly, whereas cervical spine surgery is associated with lower return-to-play rates and measurable performance declines. Linemen dominate the lumbar surgery cohort while skill-position players are overrepresented in cervical cases, suggesting position-specific rehabilitation protocols are warranted. Staff should set more cautious return-to-play expectations for players undergoing cervical procedures and prioritize targeted rehab that accounts for the different biomechanical demands placed on linemen versus skill players.
methods: Retrospective observational cohort study. Publicly available NFL injury reports 2005–2022 were searched; player injury history, position, age, BMI, procedure type, and return-to-play status were sourced from multiple sports media and analytics platforms. Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades assessed pre- and post-surgical performance. Statistical analyses used independent two-sample t-tests, two-way ANOVA, linear regression, and logistic regression via Python 3.10.
findings: 144 spine surgeries in 136 players: 77 lumbar (61% RTP, avg PFF change +6.3%) vs. 67 cervical (47% RTP, avg PFF change -5.8%). Lumbar cohort had significantly higher BMI (32.3 vs. 30.5, p=0.013) and more discectomies (75% vs. 10%). Cervical cohort had significantly more fusions (39% vs. 6.5%). Players with prior lumbar surgery had higher RTP (83% vs. 56%, p=0.020). Only 22% of cervical patients showed performance improvement vs. 58% of lumbar patients (p=0.019). Pre-injury PFF >70 was associated with greater post-surgical performance decline (F=8.917, p=0.005).
limitations: PFF data unavailable prior to 2006, limiting pre-2006 analysis. Procedure specifics and exact anatomical region were often unavailable due to NFL privacy reporting requirements. Sample included only players whose injuries were publicly reported, creating potential selection bias. Relatively small sample with performance data available for only 33 lumbar and 26 cervical patients.
practicalImplications: Team medical staff should differentiate return-to-play timelines and performance expectations based on surgery type: lumbar patients have reasonable prospects for full performance recovery, while cervical patients face higher risk of permanent performance decline. Position-specific rehabilitation plans should account for the different injury patterns—linemen (chronic wear, lumbar) versus skill-position players (acute high-impact, cervical). Pre-injury PFF performance level should be incorporated into post-surgical prognosis discussions.
athleteDev: For NFL athlete development, this study underscores that high-BMI linemen are disproportionately affected by lumbar spine injuries due to the chronic loading demands of their positions. Prehabilitation programs focusing on lumbar spine health, core stability, and load management are especially relevant for linemen. Cervical spine injury prevention through technique coaching (blocking, tackling form) is critical for skill-position players who face higher collision risk.
rtp: Lumbar surgery RTP rate of 61% (or 83% in those with prior lumbar surgery) informs realistic RTP timelines for NFL players. Cervical surgery RTP rate of only 47%, combined with average performance decrements, suggests that cervical spine surgery should trigger more extensive RTP criteria before clearance. A history of prior lumbar surgery was paradoxically associated with higher RTP rates, possibly reflecting improved patient selection and surgical/rehab advances. PFF performance trajectories post-surgery should inform RTP decisions alongside clinical benchmarks.

citation: Dupont MM, Fourman MS, Iyer S, Qureshi SA, Sheha ED, Rhie-Lee J, Dowdell J. Impact of Lumbar Disk Herniation on Performance Outcomes and New Contracts in the National Football League. Clin Spine Surg. 2022;00:000-000.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study identified 101 NFL players treated for lumbar disk herniation (LDH) between 2000 and 2020 via public records. Of these, 75 (74.3%) returned to play at preserved performance levels (mean PFF 65.6 vs. 67.9 pre-injury, P=0.2), but total contract values fell significantly from a median of $5.97M to $2.00M and guaranteed values from $1.67M to $0.47M (both P<0.01). Younger age and higher preinjury PFF grade were the primary independent predictors of both RTP and contract-signing ability, while surgery type did not significantly affect outcomes.
tldr: Most NFL players (74%) return to full performance levels after lumbar disk herniation treatment, but their earning power drops sharply regardless of whether they had surgery. The key drivers of both returning to play and signing a new contract are the player's age and their pre-injury performance level—not the type of treatment. This means medical and performance staff should focus on identifying high-value younger players for aggressive rehabilitation investment, while setting realistic financial expectations for older or lower-performing players who sustain an LDH.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using publicly available NFL injury reports and prosportstransactions.com to identify LDH cases 2000–2020. Age, BMI, position, treatment type, and PFF grades were collected. Contract values (total and guaranteed) were sourced from spotrac.com and overthecap.com. Statistical analysis used Shapiro-Wilk normality tests, paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for pre/post comparisons, and multivariable logistic regression. Data managed via REDCap.
findings: 101 players treated for LDH; 77 (76.2%) required surgery. RTP rate: 74.3%. Mean posttreatment PFF 65.6 vs. 67.9 preinjury (P=0.2, non-significant). Median total contract value decreased from $5.97M to $2.00M (P<0.01); guaranteed value from $1.67M to $0.47M (P<0.01). Independent predictors of RTP: lower age (OR=0.75, 95% CI 0.59–0.96) and higher preinjury PFF (OR=1.08, 95% CI 1.01–1.16). Independent predictors of signing a new contract: lower age (OR=0.82) and higher preinjury PFF (OR=1.11). Nonoperative patients returned to play sooner (1.8 vs. 7.9 months) but had similar career longevity and performance.
limitations: Retrospective design based on public records introduces reporting and selection bias; nonoperative players may be underrepresented in public data. Specific LDH details (e.g., free fragments) were not collected. Small sample size for contract comparisons (n=33 after excluding rookie contracts). PFF grading involves human analyst subjectivity.
practicalImplications: NFL medical and performance staff should inform players and team management that performance levels are typically preserved after LDH treatment but contract values will likely decrease. The treatment modality (surgical vs. non-surgical) should not be the primary driver of RTP decisions; rather, player age and preinjury performance level are better predictors of outcomes. Younger, higher-performing players represent the best candidates for full rehabilitation investment following LDH.
athleteDev: For NFL athlete development, LDH is a career-altering but often survivable injury from a performance standpoint. Linemen and players in positions with heavy axial loading (blocking, tackling to the torso per NFL rules) face elevated LDH risk. Preventive programming emphasizing lumbar spine stability, proper lifting mechanics in the weight room, and load monitoring during high-intensity periods may reduce LDH incidence in developing NFL players.
rtp: A 74.3% RTP rate after LDH treatment—comparable across surgical and non-surgical cohorts—provides a useful benchmark for NFL team RTP planning. Nonoperative patients return faster (median 1.8 months) while surgical patients take longer (median 7.9 months), but long-term career outcomes are similar. Age and preinjury PFF performance are the strongest clinical predictors of successful RTP, suggesting these factors should be incorporated into individualized RTP protocols and contract decisions.

citation: Nassis GP, Girard O, Chiampas GT, Krustrup P, Racinais S. In-match strategies to mitigate the effect of heat on football (soccer) players' health and performance. Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-2.
abstract: This editorial, published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine in March 2024, was prompted by anticipated extreme heat conditions at the 2026 FIFA World Cup (WBGT projected 30–35°C in six host cities). It synthesizes evidence on in-match cooling strategies for football players, noting that rectal temperatures can reach 39.5–40.2°C and muscle temperatures up to 42°C during matches in heat. The authors recommend a tiered hierarchy of cooling interventions—from whole-body cold water immersion (most effective, 0.25–0.35°C/min reduction) to ice towels, head/hand cooling, and hydration strategies—tailored to available time windows during play.
tldr: With major football tournaments increasingly scheduled in hot environments, staff need a practical cooling toolkit. Whole-body cold water immersion at half-time is the gold standard, dropping core temperature fastest, but when impractical, ice towels applied to large body surfaces are the next best option. During the brief 3-minute in-game cooling breaks, the priority should shift from core cooling (not feasible in that time) to hydration, reducing skin temperature via chilled water and ice towels around the neck, and preserving cognitive function. American football teams preparing for hot-weather games—especially in outdoor stadiums in warm climates—should adopt these same principles in halftime and timeout protocols.
methods: Editorial with narrative synthesis of evidence. The authors reviewed studies that closely replicated football match conditions (including cooling breaks and half-time) to identify the most effective in-match cooling strategies. Evidence was graded by ecological validity and applied specifically to the football context. Historical weather data (2012–2021) for 2026 World Cup host cities informed the clinical context.
findings: Core temperatures during football in heat reach 39.5–40.2°C (rectal) and up to 42°C (muscle). Cognitive decline begins above 38.7°C. Whole-body cold water immersion reduces core temperature 0.25–0.35°C/min—most effective cooling method. Ice towels over large body surface reduce temperature 0.1°C/min—second most effective. Pre-cooling combined with mid-cooling (ice towels, head/hand cooling) improves second-half sprinting. During 3-min breaks, internal temperature reduction is not feasible; focus should be hydration and skin cooling. FIFPRO recommends cooling breaks at 15th and 60th minute. Sex differences in thermoregulation may place women at greater disadvantage in dry heat.
limitations: Most evidence is based on studies involving men; applicability to women and youth players is uncertain. Limited studies have directly replicated football match conditions with cooling breaks. The editorial does not present original data. Evidence on optimal cooling for extra time and multi-match tournament schedules is lacking.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science and sports medicine staff should establish pre-game and half-time cooling protocols for hot-weather competitions. Cold water immersion tanks should be available at half-time when feasible. For American football, timeout and halftime cooling protocols using ice towels, cold water, and neck/forearm cooling can meaningfully reduce thermal strain. Medical personnel should be included in decisions about game scheduling and cooling break timing. Adjust warm-up structure in heat to minimize temperature elevation before kick-off.
athleteDev: Heat management is directly relevant to American football, particularly for summer training camps, preseason games, and warm-weather regular season matchups. Developing players' heat acclimatization (10–14 sessions maintaining core temp >38.5°C for 60 min) is a key preparatory strategy. Practice schedule design, hydration protocols, and cooling station availability should be integrated into player development programs for teams in warm climates.
rtp: Heat illness can be a cause of acute performance impairment or medical emergency requiring game removal. These cooling strategies are primarily performance-enhancing and preventive rather than RTP-focused. However, understanding the thermal load during return-to-play progressions in hot environments—and applying cooling protocols during those sessions—can protect players from heat-related setbacks during RTP from injury.

citation: Balius R, Pedret C, Mecho S, Garcia-Romero A, Yanguas J, Sala-Blanch X, Carrera A, Reina F, Kassarjian A. Injuries to accessory head of rectus femoris in professional football players: anatomical description, imaging findings, and clinical implications. Folia Morphol. 2026;85:e01726016.
abstract: This retrospective case series from 2022–2024 described seven professional footballers (5 male, 2 female) competing in Spanish or English first/second divisions who sustained injuries to a previously undescribed accessory anterolateral head of the rectus femoris (RF). MRI revealed an accessory muscle belly with distinct proximal and distal aponeuroses separate from the main RF, with all injuries occurring at the myotendinous junction. Median return-to-play was 8 weeks (range 15–90 days), with isolated accessory head injuries averaging 27.2 days. Cadaveric dissection of a 68-year-old male confirmed an accessory RF belly 15 cm in length arising anterolateral to the main RF and merging with the quadriceps tendon.
tldr: A previously undescribed anatomical variant—an accessory anterolateral head of the rectus femoris—can be injured in kicking athletes and may be misidentified as a standard RF injury or missed entirely on imaging. Recognizing this variant on MRI or ultrasound allows clinicians to more accurately classify injury severity, set appropriate RTP timelines (isolated injuries heal faster, ~27 days, vs. concurrent RF injuries that can take up to 90 days), and avoid unnecessary concern about what might appear to be a more extensive injury. Sports medicine staff working with kickers, punters, or any athlete performing repetitive explosive hip flexion should be aware of this variant.
methods: Retrospective case series approved by the Catalan Sports Council Ethics Committee (37/2024/CEICEGC). Seven professional footballers with MRI-confirmed accessory RF head injuries 2022–2024 were included. MRI (T1W and T2W fat-saturated sequences, axial and coronal planes) characterized each lesion. Age, sex, injured limb, injury mechanism, lesion location, RF involvement, and RTP time were recorded. Cadaveric dissection of a fresh-frozen donated specimen provided anatomical confirmation.
findings: Seven cases identified: five males, two females, ages 19–32. All lesions on lateral aspect of RF. MRI revealed accessory anterolateral RF belly with distinct proximal aponeurosis fusing with RF central septum. All injuries at myotendinous junction of accessory head. Mean RTP: 38.7 days (range 15–90). Excluding cases with concurrent RF injuries: mean RTP 27.2 days (range 15–35). Three cases had concurrent central RF aponeurosis injuries. One reinjury occurred (Case 3, hypertrophic scar). Cadaveric accessory belly: 15 cm length, 75 mm intramuscular tendon, maximum width 16 mm, anatomical pattern consistent with MRI findings.
limitations: Very small sample (n=7), limiting conclusions about incidence and prognosis. Study population limited to professional footballers in Spanish and English leagues; generalizability to other sports or American football populations uncertain. Single cadaver dissection provides limited anatomical frequency data. Retrospective design.
practicalImplications: Sports medicine practitioners should be aware of this RF variant when evaluating anterior thigh injuries in kicking athletes. MRI or ultrasound cross-sectional imaging can identify the accessory head, especially in well-developed muscles. Correct identification prevents overestimation of injury severity (the accessory head injury alone carries a shorter RTP than a true RF injury) and enables targeted rehabilitation. When imaging shows apparent RF involvement on the lateral aspect, closer inspection for accessory aponeurotic structures is warranted.
athleteDev: In American football, NFL kickers and punters are at particular risk for RF and accessory RF injuries given the explosive hip flexion demands of their technique. This anatomical variant likely exists in American football players as well. Imaging protocols for anterior thigh injuries in kickers and skill players should include awareness of this variant to avoid misclassification. Strength and conditioning programming for kickers should address the eccentric loading demands on the entire rectus femoris complex.
rtp: Isolated accessory RF head injuries have a favorable RTP prognosis (~27 days), while concurrent central RF aponeurosis involvement extends RTP considerably (up to 90 days). Accurate MRI identification of whether the main RF is also injured is therefore critical for RTP timeline planning. The 8-week median RTP for the full series (including concurrent injuries) provides a conservative upper bound for clinical planning when imaging cannot clearly differentiate accessory-only from combined injuries.

citation: Vermeulen R, van Dyk N, Whiteley R, Chamari K, Gregson W, Lolli L, Bahr R, Tol JL, Serner A. Injury-inciting circumstances of sudden-onset hamstring injuries: video analyses of 63 match injuries in male professional football players in the Qatar Stars League (2013–2020). Br J Sports Med. 2024;0:1-9.
abstract: This single-centre observational cohort study used systematic video analysis to describe and categorise the injury-inciting circumstances of 63 sudden-onset hamstring match injuries in male professional football players from the Qatar Stars League (2013–2020). Nine investigators independently analysed broadcasted match footage, categorising injuries by playing situation, player actions, and other considerations (contact, balance). Running was involved in 86% of injuries, with the most common single-player action being short-distance acceleration (0–10 m, 24% of all injuries). Pressing, indirect contact, and inadequate balance were frequently involved, particularly in short-distance acceleration injuries.
tldr: Contrary to the prevailing belief that hamstring injuries in football primarily occur during high-speed sprinting, this video analysis found that the most common single injury scenario is short-distance acceleration (under 10 meters), often combined with pressing, indirect contact, and poor balance. This has direct implications for American football: rehabilitation and injury prevention programs focused solely on max-velocity running work may be incomplete. Training drills that replicate chaotic, contested short accelerations—such as press coverage, pass rush, and route running against defenders—may better address the full spectrum of hamstring injury risk.
methods: Single-centre observational cohort study using a prospective injury surveillance database (Aspetar Injury and Illness Surveillance Programme). 295 sudden-onset hamstring injuries registered 2013–2020; 131 had available video footage; 63 had a clear painful event and were included. Nine investigators (physiotherapists, sports medicine doctors, sports scientists) independently scored each video using a standardised scoring form covering playing situation, player actions (running type/speed/distance, kicking, jumping, pressing, contact, balance), and other considerations. Consensus required simple majority (5/9 investigators). Classification tree analysis performed in Orange software.
findings: 86% of injuries involved running. Most common single-player action: acceleration 0–10 m (24% of all injuries). Running speed was moderate in 52% of cases. Pressing involved in 46% of injuries; indirect contact in 27%; inadequate balance in 41%. At 0–10 m acceleration: indirect contact (53%) and inadequate balance (67%) frequently present. Injuries at high-speed over longer distances (20+ m) occurred in 16% of cases with less contact and better balance. Non-running injuries: kicking (10%), jumping (5%). No direct contact injuries observed. 56% occurred in second half.
limitations: Video analysis cannot precisely determine the exact moment of injury; actions leading up to injury were still determined by analyst opinion. No formal rater reliability testing performed. Only 48% of injuries with available video had a clear painful event identifiable, potentially missing more subtle onset injuries. Study limited to male professional football players; generalizability to women, other levels, age groups, and American football uncertain. Framework largely based on rugby consensus, as a football-specific consensus was published after the study.
practicalImplications: Injury prevention and rehabilitation programs for hamstring injuries should extend beyond high-speed running to include contested short accelerations, pressing scenarios, and drills involving indirect contact and balance challenges. Small-sided games that replicate these chaotic situations may address a broader injury risk profile than isolated sprint training. Kicking mechanics should also be included in hamstring injury prevention given their consistent 10–11% contribution to hamstring injuries across multiple studies.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, this study challenges the assumption that hamstring injury risk is primarily a high-speed running problem. Defensive backs in press coverage, linebackers in pursuit angles, and running backs making short cuts all perform the contested short accelerations identified as the highest-risk scenario. Position-specific drills that train explosive acceleration mechanics in congested spaces—while emphasizing body control and balance—should be incorporated into hamstring injury prevention programs.
rtp: During hamstring RTP progressions, re-exposure to the full range of injury-inciting circumstances is essential before clearance. Return-to-running programs focused solely on linear speed work may not adequately prepare players for the contested, multidirectional, contact-influenced accelerations that produce most match-play hamstring injuries. RTP protocols should include sport-specific scenarios involving pressing, change of direction, and balance challenges at progressive intensities before full clearance for match play.
